passageSets.title: The Voice That Cannot Be Trusted
passageSets.passage:
When a novel is narrated in the first person, readers tend to extend the speaker a provisional trust. The voice that says 'I' appears to report from inside the events it describes, and such nearness feels like a warrant of accuracy. Some of the most rewarding fiction, however, is built on the slow betrayal of that warrant. The unreliable narrator, a teller whose account we gradually learn to doubt, converts reading into an act of quiet detection, in which the truer story must be reassembled from the seams, the evasions, and the contradictions of the story we are actually given.

The device works because it exploits a habit we cannot easily switch off. We are disposed to believe a coherent voice, especially one that confides in us, and a skilled author lets that disposition run ahead of the evidence. A narrator may insist on his own generosity while every remembered scene shows him grasping; another may describe herself as perfectly calm while her sentences tighten and repeat. The reader who notices the gap is rewarded with a second narrative, richer than the first, that the narrator never meant to tell. Meaning arrives not from what is said but from the distance between the saying and the seen.

It is tempting to conclude that the unreliable narrator proves all narration suspect, that every 'I' is a mask and every account a distortion. But this reading proves too much. If a narrator were unreliable in every particular, the reader would have no fixed points from which to measure the distortion, and the technique would collapse into noise. Unreliability is legible only against a background the reader can reconstruct as true. The author must therefore plant reliable signals, of tone, of fact, of other characters' reactions, so that the narrator's swerves from them register as swerves rather than as the world itself.

This is why the finest examples never simply expose the narrator as a liar. They hold two accounts in suspension, the narrator's and the reader's, and let the friction between them illuminate something neither could state alone. We come to understand not only what happened but why the teller needed to tell it otherwise, and that need, that self-protecting distortion, becomes the deepest subject of the book. The narrator's errors are not obstacles to the story; they are the story, provided the reader is given just enough to see past them.

Seen this way, unreliable narration is less a trick than a model of how understanding ordinarily works. We rarely receive the world plainly. We receive it through someone's interested account, our own included, and the labor of interpretation is the labor of correcting for that interest without pretending we can dispense with accounts altogether. The novel that teaches us to read its narrator against the grain is teaching a skill we need well beyond the page, in every room where a confident voice asks to be believed.

passageSets.title: The Self That Persists
passageSets.passage:
You are, in some obvious sense, the same person who fell asleep last night, the same person who was a child decades ago. Yet almost nothing about you has stayed fixed. The matter of your body has been replaced many times over; your beliefs, your tastes, even your personality have shifted, sometimes beyond recognition. In what, then, does your being one continuing person consist? The question is not idle wordplay. Our practices of promising, blame, gratitude, and grief all assume that the person now present is the same one who acted, or was wronged, before.

An old answer locates identity in some unchanging core, a soul or essence that persists beneath the flux. Whatever alters, this substance remains, and it is what makes the aged person identical to the child. The proposal has a certain comfort, but it explains little. If the enduring core has no observable features, if it is exactly the same whether one becomes generous or cruel, wise or foolish, then it does no work in accounting for the identity we actually care about, which is bound up with precisely those changing features. An identity that would survive the total replacement of everything we recognize is not the identity whose loss we mourn.

A more promising answer looks not for a thing that stays the same but for a pattern of connection across time. What makes the later person continuous with the earlier is not an unchanging substance but a web of links: memories that reach back, intentions carried forward, character gradually transformed by degrees rather than all at once. On this view the self is less like a stone that endures than like a rope, whose unity comes not from any single fiber running its whole length but from the overlapping of many shorter fibers. No one strand need persist for the rope to be one rope.

This picture fits our practices better, but it too has its puzzles. If identity is a matter of connection by degrees, then it seems to come in degrees, and we are reluctant to say that someone is only partly the person who made a promise. Cases can be imagined, gradual and drastic change, in which there is simply no determinate answer to whether the later person is the earlier one, and our all-or-nothing concepts of responsibility strain against such gradations. The rope may fray in ways that leave us genuinely unsure how many ropes we have.

Perhaps the deepest suggestion is that the self is less a fact we discover than a work we sustain. We are the authors, not merely the observers, of our continuity: we knit the strands together by remembering, by keeping commitments, by telling a story in which today's self inherits yesterday's. This does not make identity arbitrary, for the story must answer to what actually happened and can fail. But it does make personal identity more like an achievement than a given, something enacted rather than simply found, and it locates the persistence of the self in an ongoing activity rather than in any changeless thing.

questions.stem: The primary purpose of the passage is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: prove that personal identity is an illusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that an unchanging soul is the only coherent basis for identity

choices.label: C
choices.text: examine competing accounts of what makes a person the same over time and favor a connection-based, narrative view

choices.label: D
choices.text: show that our practices of blame and gratitude are unjustified

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author surveys the 'unchanging core' view and the 'pattern of connection' view, favoring the latter and its narrative development. A is wrong because the author treats identity as a real achievement, not an illusion. B is wrong because the author says the unchanging-core view 'explains little.' D is wrong because the author takes those practices seriously as things a theory of identity must accommodate, not as unjustified.

passageSets.title: The Performer at the Desk
passageSets.passage:
Nothing seems more humble than the work of the translator, and nothing is more routinely misunderstood. The common picture imagines the translator as a kind of glass through which a foreign text passes unchanged, so that the ideal translation would be the one we forget we are reading. On this view the translator's single duty is fidelity, and every departure from the original counts as a small betrayal. The picture is tidy, flattering to readers, and almost entirely wrong.

Consider what fidelity would require. A sentence in one language carries not only its plain sense but a music, a set of echoes, a weight of idiom that no other language reproduces exactly. The translator who preserves the literal meaning may lose the rhythm; the one who preserves the rhythm may bend the sense. A pun that turns on two meanings of a single word simply has no counterpart elsewhere, and the translator must choose which thread to follow and which to cut. Faced with these forced choices, the translator cannot merely copy; he must decide what in the original matters most and rebuild it with different materials. Translation, in short, is less like copying a painting than like performing a score: the notes are given, but the sound is made anew each time.

Once we see translation as performance, the cult of the invisible translator looks not humble but dishonest. Every translation embodies a reading, a set of judgments about what the text is chiefly doing, and to pretend otherwise is to smuggle interpretation in under the cover of mere transmission. The honest translator, by contrast, accepts that a translation is a new work answerable to two masters at once: the original it serves and the language it must now live in. A version that reads as though it were written in the new language, yet still bears the pressure of the foreign, is not a betrayal of the original but its fullest afterlife.

This does not mean that anything goes, and here the argument must guard against its own excess. To say that translation is creative is not to license the translator to substitute his own preferences for the author's, nor to treat the source as a mere occasion for display. The freedom is a freedom to serve well, not a freedom from service. A translation that abandons the sense of the original in pursuit of a pretty phrase has not performed the score; it has thrown it away and improvised. The difference between interpretation and invention is real, even when it is hard to locate, and the good translator lives precisely on that uneasy border.

What follows is a paradox worth sitting with. The translations we most admire are often the least faithful in the narrow sense and the most faithful in the deep one, capturing what a merely literal version would let slip. The so-called untranslatable, the word or joke with no equivalent, does not defeat translation; it is the very place where the translator's art becomes visible, forcing a solution that a native writer would never have needed to find. To read a great translation is thus to read two authors at once, the first speaking through the second, and to mistake this doubleness for a flaw is to misunderstand the whole enterprise. The translator is not a pane of glass but a performer, and we are the better for hearing the performance.

passageSets.title: The Problem of Moral Luck
passageSets.passage:
We ordinarily believe that people can be blamed only for what lies within their control. It would be monstrous to condemn someone for the color of her eyes or the country of her birth, since she chose neither. This principle, that responsibility extends no further than the will, feels almost self-evident, and much of our moral thinking presupposes it. Yet our actual judgments violate it constantly, and the gap between the principle and the practice is the puzzle philosophers have come to call moral luck.

Consider two equally careless drivers. Both glance at their phones for the same reckless instant; but one happens to strike a child who has stepped into the road, while the other, by sheer chance, finds the road empty. The first will be treated as a near-murderer, by the law and by his own conscience; the second will merely feel he had a scare. Their wills were identical; only luck distinguishes them. If blame truly tracked control alone, we would judge the two exactly alike, yet almost no one does. The outcome, entirely beyond the drivers' control once the phone was lifted, transforms how we assess them.

The same disturbing pattern appears wherever we look. Whether the courage in a person is ever tested depends on whether history hands him a crisis; the very desires and dispositions he acts from were shaped by genes and an upbringing he did not choose. Push the point far enough and the domain of the purely voluntary, the part of a life for which luck is supposedly irrelevant, shrinks toward a vanishing sliver. If we subtract everything that luck contributed, it is unclear that anything recognizably a person remains to be praised or blamed at all.

One response, associated with a stern strand of moral philosophy, tries to quarantine the will from luck entirely. On this view, what has genuine moral worth is only the inner act of willing, the good intention, which no misfortune can touch; the driver who kills and the driver who does not are equally guilty, and the difference in outcome is morally irrelevant however loudly our feelings protest. This has the virtue of consistency, and it honors the control principle without flinching. But it does so at a steep price, for it declares vast stretches of our moral life, our sense that consequences matter, that a life can be tragic, that some are simply lucky, to be one long mistake.

Perhaps the honest conclusion is that we are caught. The control principle is not a rule we can casually drop, for it expresses something we deeply believe about fairness. Yet the luck-drenched judgments it condemns are not a superficial error either; they run through our praise, our blame, our law, and our self-assessment too thoroughly to be excised. We seem to hold two convictions that cannot both be fully honored, and the discomfort this produces is not a problem awaiting a clever solution so much as a permanent feature of the moral landscape. To see moral luck clearly is not to resolve it but to lose the comfortable illusion that our moral judgments rest on a single, consistent foundation.

passageSets.title: The Contract of Genre
passageSets.passage:
Critics who prize originality often treat genre fiction as a lesser thing, a factory product stamped out to a template while real literature invents its own form with each new book. The detective story must have its corpse, its suspects, its investigator, and its revelation; the romance its obstacle and its union. Where, the objection runs, is the room for art in a form whose ending is known before the first page is read? To write within a genre, on this view, is to trade the freedom of the artist for the security of the formula.

The objection mistakes the nature of the bargain. Genre is best understood not as a cage but as a contract, a set of expectations shared in advance by writer and reader, and it is precisely this shared expectation that makes certain pleasures possible. A reader who knows the conventions of the detective story knows that the clues are there to be found and that the solution will be both surprising and, in retrospect, fair. That knowledge is not a spoiler; it is the very ground on which suspense is built. Surprise is only possible against a background of expectation, and genre supplies the background. Remove the convention and you do not liberate the reader; you leave her with nothing to be surprised against.

Nor does the contract dictate the outcome in any way that matters. That the criminal will be caught tells us almost nothing about how a particular novel will move, whom it will make us suspect, or what it will show us of greed, or grief, or a town's hidden life along the way. The known ending is a frame, and a frame does not paint the picture. Within it a writer has room for everything that distinguishes one book from another: voice, character, the pacing of revelation, the moral weather of a world. The constraint does not compete with these; it is what lets them register.

The best writers in a genre go further, using the reader's knowledge of the rules as an instrument. A convention the audience expects can be delayed, inverted, or honored in a way that quietly comments on itself, and none of these moves would mean anything to a reader who did not carry the expectation in the first place. The twist that upends a formula depends on the formula; the parody depends on the thing parodied. Far from limiting invention, the rules give the writer a second layer to work on, the layer of the reader's anticipation, which exists only because the genre established it.

This is not to deny that most genre writing is routine, for most writing of every kind is routine, and a convention can certainly become a crutch for those with nothing else to offer. The point is that the fault, when it occurs, lies in the writer and not in the form. A shared set of expectations is a resource, and like any resource it can be squandered or used well. To dismiss genre as such is to confuse the instrument with the poor players who sometimes take it up, and to miss that the very constraints so often blamed for mediocrity are the ones that make the finest examples possible.

passageSets.title: The Weight of Doing
passageSets.passage:
Most people feel a difference between harming someone and merely failing to help, between pushing a man into the path of danger and declining to pull him from it. The law marks the distinction, common speech marks it, and conscience marks it most of all: we recoil more sharply from the thought of killing than from the thought of letting die, even when the person ends up equally dead. Whether this felt difference reflects a real moral one, or merely a bias we would abandon on reflection, is among the sharpest questions in ethics.

Those who defend the distinction point to agency. When I harm, I insert myself as the cause of a misfortune that would not otherwise have occurred; when I merely fail to prevent a harm, the cause lies elsewhere, and I have at most declined to interrupt it. To treat the two as identical, they argue, is to make each of us responsible for every evil we could conceivably have prevented, an impossible standard that would erase the space in which any life could be lived. The distinction, on this view, protects a boundary around the self without which morality would become tyrannical.

The critic answers with a pointed question: to the victim, what difference does it make? A death is a death, and if the outcome is the same and the effort required to prevent it is trivial, then the insistence that doing is worse than allowing can look like a comfortable excuse for inaction, a way of purchasing clean hands at the price of another's life. The person who could save a drowning child at no risk, and does not, has surely done a grave wrong, whatever the metaphysics of causation may say about who set the tragedy in motion.

The most defensible position may grant force to both sides rather than crowning either. The distinction between doing and allowing does seem to track something real, some genuine difference in how an outcome relates to an agent's will, and a morality that ignored it entirely would indeed be unlivable. But the difference is a matter of degree and defeasible weight, not an absolute barrier; it can be outweighed when the harm allowed is great and the cost of preventing it is small. To let a child drown to keep one's shoes dry is not excused by the observation that one did not push him.

What the debate finally teaches is caution about our own intuitions. The felt gap between killing and letting die is strong enough to demand respect and unreliable enough to demand scrutiny, and the task is neither to worship it as bedrock nor to dismiss it as illusion, but to ask, in each case, whether it is doing honest work or merely licensing a convenient indifference. Our moral reactions are evidence, but they are not verdicts.

What makes the question so stubborn is that our reactions are at once too strong to ignore and too unreliable to obey without thought. The felt gap between killing and letting die is not nothing; it tracks a real difference in how an outcome flows from a will. Yet the same feeling can be enlisted to excuse a comfortable passivity, letting a great harm proceed because we did not set it going. To treat the intuition as evidence rather than verdict, weighing in each case whether it is doing honest work, is the only path between worship and dismissal.

passageSets.title: Carrying a Poem Across
passageSets.passage:
We tend to praise a translation by calling it faithful, as though the translator's task were the same as a witness's under oath: report the words, add nothing, omit nothing. The image is comforting and almost entirely wrong. Words do not travel between languages the way coins travel between pockets, keeping their value as they change hands. A word carries a history of associations, a music, a weight upon the tongue, and none of these survive a simple exchange. To render each word by its dictionary partner is not to be faithful; it is to produce a text that is accurate in its parts and dead as a whole.

Consider what a poem actually does. It makes meaning inseparable from sound, so that a line's rhythm is part of its argument and a rhyme can feel like a small proof. A translator who preserves the literal sense while discarding the music has kept the least translatable thing and thrown away the reason the poem was written. The alternative is to ask what the original does to a reader who knows its language from birth, and then to build, out of the new language's own resources, something that does the same. This is not copying; it is re-creation, and it may require changing an image to keep a feeling, or breaking a literal sense to keep a truth.

Here a reasonable worry arises. If the translator is licensed to change images and recast lines, what protects the original from simply being overwritten by the translator's preferences? A bad translator, granted such freedom, produces not the foreign poet but himself, wearing the foreign poet's name. The danger is real, and the history of translation is littered with versions that tell us more about the translator's era and vanity than about the work they claim to carry across.

The answer is that re-creation is disciplined by a purpose, and the purpose is not self-expression. The translator's allegiance is owed neither to the individual words nor to his own voice, but to the experience the original produces. Every liberty must be earned by that standard: does this change bring the reader nearer to what the first reader felt, or does it merely flatter the translator? Fidelity, properly understood, is fidelity to effect, and it is far more demanding than literalism, because it cannot be achieved by looking up words. It requires the translator first to understand the poem as a critic does, and then to remake it as an artist must.

Seen this way, translation is criticism that dares to take a risk. The literalist hides behind the dictionary and calls his caution respect; the true translator exposes an interpretation, line by line, and can be caught being wrong. That vulnerability is the price of doing the thing at all. A translation is always a reading, never a mirror, and the reader who forgets this asks for an impossibility and rewards the translator who pretends to supply it. The best we can hope for is not the original, which stays behind in its own language, but a new poem that remembers where it came from.

passageSets.title: The Desk and the Dream
passageSets.passage:
Set aside, for a moment, your certainty that the desk before you is real. You cannot step outside your own mind to compare your image of the desk with the desk itself; all you ever have is the experience, and the experience would be exactly the same whether a desk caused it or something else did. A vivid dream can present a desk as convincingly as waking life, and while dreaming you do not doubt it. If your evidence for the outer world is only how things seem, and how things seem could in principle be produced without any world at all, then it appears you cannot rule out that you are deceived. So runs the skeptic's challenge, and its power lies in how little it has to assume.

The natural first response is to protest that the challenge is absurd, that no one really doubts the desk, and that a philosophy contradicting universal conviction has refuted itself. But this reply mistakes the skeptic's aim. He does not predict that you will doubt the desk; he grants that you cannot help believing in it. His claim is narrower and more troubling: that you cannot justify the belief, that a conviction you are unable to shake may nonetheless be one you are unable to support. Psychological certainty and rational entitlement are not the same, and the gap between them is exactly where the challenge lives.

A more serious reply concedes that certainty is unavailable but denies that certainty was ever the right standard. Perhaps knowledge does not require ruling out every conceivable deception, only believing what is in fact true on grounds that are, in the ordinary run of things, reliable. If my senses generally track the world, then my belief in the desk can count as knowledge even though I cannot prove, from the inside, that I am not dreaming. On this view the skeptic wins a hollow victory, establishing only that we lack a kind of certainty we never needed.

Yet the skeptic has a rejoinder that keeps the problem alive. To say that my senses are 'in fact reliable' is to help myself to a claim about the very world whose existence is in question; I cannot confirm the reliability of my senses without using those senses, and the circle closes. The comfortable reply, he says, assumes precisely what he asked us to justify, and assuming the world in order to vouch for our knowledge of it is not an argument but a shrug.

The honest upshot is not that we should doubt the desk, which we cannot and need not do, but that we should be humbler about what our knowledge rests on. The challenge may have no refutation that does not, somewhere, lean on the world it was asked to secure. Living confidently within beliefs we cannot ultimately ground from the outside may simply be the human condition, and recognizing this is less a defeat than a lesson in the limits of what reasoning about our own experience can deliver.

The honest residue of the argument is neither doubt about the desk nor triumph over the skeptic but a chastened sense of what our knowledge rests upon. We cannot step outside our own experience to check it against the world, and every attempt to vouch for our senses must, somewhere, use them. To live confidently within beliefs we cannot ground from the outside may simply be the human condition, and seeing this clearly is not a defeat for reason but a lesson in its limits, one that leaves the desk exactly where it was while unsettling only our pretension to certainty.

passageSets.title: The Trustworthy Liar
passageSets.passage:
When a storyteller cannot be trusted, readers tend to feel cheated, as though a contract had been broken. Yet the narrator who misreports, conceals, or misunderstands the events he describes is among the oldest and most fertile devices in prose fiction. The so-called unreliable narrator is not a defect in craft but a deliberate arrangement, one that asks the reader to hold two stories at once: the tale the narrator means to tell, and the different tale that leaks through the cracks of his telling. The pleasure, and the difficulty, lie in the distance between the two.

Critics often describe this distance as a puzzle to be solved. On this account, the reader's task is to strip away the narrator's distortions and reconstruct what really happened, as a detective reassembles a crime from unreliable witnesses. There is something to this. A narrator who insists too loudly on his own innocence invites us to suspect guilt, and half the art of such fiction is the planting of clues that the speaker does not know he has dropped. Read this way, the novel becomes a contest between author and reader conducted over the narrator's head.

But the puzzle model, taken alone, mistakes the means for the end. If the only reward were a hidden set of facts, the unreliable narrator would be a clumsy way to deliver them, and a second reading, once the trick is known, would be pointless. In practice the opposite is true: such books reward rereading precisely because the gap between the narrator's account and our own is not a lock to be picked but a space to be inhabited. We come to understand why the narrator sees as he does, what his blindness costs him, and how easily we might share it. The distortion is not noise to be filtered out; it is the portrait itself.

This is why the device carries a moral charge that a merely reliable narration cannot. To read against a narrator is to practice a kind of double vision, sympathy and suspicion held together, that ordinary life rarely permits. We are placed inside a mind we can also judge, and the judgment is complicated by the intimacy. A cruel narrator rendered from the outside is easy to condemn; the same cruelty, voiced from within by someone who believes himself kind, implicates the reader who has been nodding along. The discomfort is the point.

None of this means that anything goes, that every reading is as good as any other. A narrator's unreliability is itself constructed with care, and the text sets limits on what we may plausibly infer. The author who abandons those limits produces not richness but mere confusion, a narration unreliable in no particular direction and therefore about nothing. The achievement lies in control: in building a voice wrong in exactly the ways that matter, so that its errors compose a coherent second meaning. To call such a narrator simply a liar is to miss the design, for the most revealing narrators are often the ones most sincerely convinced that they are telling the truth.

passageSets.title: The Riddle of Tomorrow
passageSets.passage:
We assume, in nearly everything we do, that the future will resemble the past. We expect bread to nourish because it always has, the sun to rise because it always has, dropped objects to fall because they always have. This expectation is the silent engine of all practical life and of science itself, which generalizes from observed cases to unobserved ones as a matter of course. Yet when a famous skeptic asked what justifies the assumption, he found, to his own discomfort, that the answer is far harder to give than anyone expects.

The trouble is this. Why believe the future will resemble the past? The obvious reply is that it always has: past futures, so to speak, have reliably resembled their pasts. But notice what this argument does. It justifies relying on regularity by appealing to the fact that reliance on regularity has worked before, which is to use the very principle in question as its own support. To argue that induction is trustworthy because it has been trustworthy is to reason in a circle, assuming what one set out to prove. The principle cannot be established by experience, because every appeal to experience already presupposes it.

Nor can the principle be established by pure logic, for there is no contradiction in imagining a world that changes its habits tomorrow. That bread has always nourished does not make it self-contradictory to suppose it might poison next week; the supposition is strange, but it is not incoherent. And if the uniformity of nature is neither a truth of logic nor a conclusion we can draw from experience without circularity, then our most basic and indispensable form of reasoning appears to rest on no rational foundation at all. This is the riddle, and it has never been fully dissolved.

Various escapes have been attempted. Some argue that to call a belief rational just is to say it is formed by induction, so that demanding a further justification misunderstands the word. Others observe that we cannot help reasoning this way, and that a principle we are built to rely on needs no license from philosophy to keep operating. Still others accept the skeptical conclusion and add, with a shrug, that induction remains the best policy available even if it cannot be proved, since no alternative has any better credentials. Each reply has its defenders, and none has silenced the others.

What survives the debate is a lesson in humility rather than a cause for paralysis. We will go on expecting the sun to rise, and we are surely right to, for to abandon induction would be not wisdom but madness. The riddle does not counsel us to stop reasoning from experience; it reveals that this reasoning, on which everything depends, is something we practice without being able to ground, a faith so deep that we mistake it for a certainty. To see that clearly is not to weaken the practice but to understand its true character.

The riddle's lasting gift is humility rather than paralysis. We will go on expecting the sun to rise and the bread to nourish, and we are surely right to, for to abandon reasoning from experience would be not wisdom but madness. What the puzzle reveals is only that this reasoning, on which nearly everything depends, is something we practice without being able to justify from outside it, a reliance so deep that we mistake it for certainty. To see that clearly does not weaken the practice; it merely tells the truth about the ground on which we all, unavoidably, stand.

passageSets.title: Carried Across
passageSets.passage:
The word translation comes from a root meaning to carry across, and the metaphor quietly shapes how we judge the practice. If a text is a cargo to be ferried from one shore to another, then the translator's duty is obvious: deliver the goods intact, add nothing, lose nothing, and above all keep out of the way. By this standard the ideal translator is invisible, a pane of clear glass through which the original shines undistorted. Reviewers still praise a translation for being faithful, as though fidelity were a simple matter of not tampering with what was entrusted.

The trouble is that languages are not neutral containers holding the same goods in different wrappers. Each carves the world along its own joints. A word that seems to have an exact counterpart drags behind it a train of associations, rhythms, and histories that its supposed equivalent does not share. To render a poem's plain sense while discarding its music is to deliver the cargo and sink the ship. Faced with this, the translator cannot simply transfer; she must decide, again and again, which of a phrase's many effects to preserve and which to sacrifice, knowing that no choice saves them all. These decisions are not mechanical. They are acts of interpretation and, finally, of composition.

If that is so, the demand for invisibility begins to look less like humility than like a convenient fiction. It lets readers enjoy the fruits of a second writer's labor while crediting them entirely to the first, and it lets the translator disown responsibility for choices that are unmistakably hers. A translation is not a copy of a work but a new performance of it, closer to a pianist's interpretation of a score than to a photocopy of a page. We do not fault a pianist for having a style; we expect one, and we judge the reading by its intelligence and feeling, not by its transparency.

Here a defender of fidelity might push back, and the objection has force. If translators are authors, what protects a text from being remade in the translator's image, its meaning bent to suit a fashion or an agenda? Surely some renderings are simply wrong, misreadings that no talk of interpretation can excuse. This is true, and it marks the real limit of the argument. To say a translation is a performance is not to say that anything goes. A pianist who ignores the score is not interpreting it but replacing it, and a translator who disregards the original's constraints has stopped translating.

The resolution lies in seeing fidelity and creativity as partners rather than rivals. The faithful translator is not the one who vanishes but the one who submits to the discipline of the original while accepting that meeting its demands requires invention. Fidelity, properly understood, is not the absence of choice but the responsible exercise of it, answerable to the source yet honest about the new thing being made. To carry a text across, it turns out, is not to keep it unchanged but to build a second vessel seaworthy in different waters. The reader who ignores the name on the translator's title page is thanking the wrong shore, and mistaking a considerable art for a mere convenience.

passageSets.title: Does Everyone Act Selfishly?
passageSets.passage:
A cynical thesis enjoys perennial popularity: that beneath every apparently generous act lies a selfish motive, that no one ever really acts for another's sake, and that even the martyr and the philanthropist are, at bottom, pleasing themselves. The soldier who falls on the grenade, the parent who sacrifices for a child, the stranger who runs into danger to save another, all are said to be secretly serving their own satisfaction, whether the warm glow of virtue or the avoidance of guilt. The thesis flatters the worldly with an air of having seen through appearances.

The argument usually turns on a single move: since a person who acts does so because he wants to, and since getting what one wants is satisfying, every act is really aimed at the agent's own satisfaction. The generous man gives because he wants to give; wanting to give, he is pleased to give; therefore his giving is, in the end, for his own pleasure. Stated this way, the thesis can seem irrefutable, a truth that follows from the very nature of wanting, and its defenders press it as a hard-headed discovery about human nature.

But the argument proves far less than it claims, and its air of profundity depends on a confusion. That I act on my own desire is trivially true and says nothing about the object of that desire. The whole question is what I want, and there is all the difference in the world between wanting another's good and wanting my own pleasure. The satisfaction I feel on helping a friend is a consequence of caring about my friend; it is not the thing I was after. To infer, from the fact that satisfaction follows a fulfilled desire, that satisfaction was the desire's aim, is to reverse cause and effect.

Indeed, the very pleasure the cynic points to depends on genuinely unselfish desire. If I did not really care about my friend's welfare, and wanted only my own good feeling, then helping him would not produce the feeling, for the glow of having helped comes precisely from having wanted, for his sake, the thing that helping achieved. The self-interested account thus undermines itself: it must smuggle in the very other-directed concern it set out to deny, since without that concern there would be no satisfaction for selfishness to claim.

None of this proves that people are mostly generous, which is a separate question to be settled by observation rather than by argument. Human beings are surely a mixture, capable of real selfishness and real generosity by turns. What the analysis shows is narrower but important: that the sweeping claim that everyone must act selfishly is not a deep truth about the psyche but a muddle, one that mistakes the trivial fact that we act on our own desires for the substantive and false claim that all our desires are for ourselves.

The sweeping thesis collapses because it mistakes a triviality for a discovery. That I act on my own desire tells us nothing about what I desire, and there is all the difference in the world between wanting my own pleasure and wanting a friend's good, from which pleasure may follow as a consequence rather than an aim. Whether people are in fact mostly generous or mostly selfish is a real question, to be settled by observing how they behave; but it cannot be settled in advance by a verbal trick that quietly assumes the very self-interest it claims to have proven.

passageSets.title: More Than Decoration
passageSets.passage:
Metaphor is often treated as a kind of ornament, a decorative flourish that a writer adds to dress up a plain thought. On this familiar view, to say that an argument 'collapsed' or that time 'flew' is merely a colorful way of saying something that could, in principle, be said literally; the metaphor is a detachable garment, pleasant but inessential, and a sufficiently careful writer could always strip it away and state the bare meaning underneath. Metaphor, so understood, belongs to the poet and the advertiser, not to the serious business of thinking.

A growing body of reflection suggests this picture has things backward. Far from being a mere decoration laid over literal thought, metaphor may be one of the basic ways the mind grasps anything at all, especially anything abstract. We understand time in terms of space, speaking of the days ahead and the years behind; we understand argument in terms of war, attacking positions and defending claims; we understand ideas in terms of food, finding some half-baked and others hard to swallow. These are not occasional embellishments but pervasive patterns, and the abstractions they help us think would be hard to grasp without them.

If that is right, then metaphors do real cognitive work, and they do it partly by hiding as much as they reveal. To conceive of argument as war is to see the opponent as an enemy, the goal as victory, the concession as defeat, and to overlook the possibility that an argument might be a collaboration in which both parties end up wiser. Every metaphor lights up certain features of its subject and casts others into shadow, and because we rarely notice we are using one, its selective emphasis can shape our thinking without our consent. The metaphor we live by quietly tells us what to see.

This gives the choice of metaphor a significance the ornamental view misses entirely. To describe disease as an invader to be fought, or a market as a natural organism, or the mind as a computer, is not merely to speak vividly but to frame the whole subject in a way that suggests some questions and suppresses others. Changing the metaphor can change the thinking: to speak of a debate as a dance rather than a battle is to invite a different set of moves. Those who supply a culture's metaphors, its journalists, its teachers, its storytellers, exert a quiet influence over how it reasons.

None of this means we could or should do without metaphor, which would be like proposing to think without concepts. The lesson is rather that metaphor is not the opposite of clear thought but one of its instruments, to be chosen with the care we give any tool that shapes what it touches. To notice the metaphors we live by is not to escape them, which is impossible, but to hold them a little more lightly, alert to what each one shows us and to what it quietly leaves in the dark.

If metaphor is an instrument of thought rather than an ornament laid over it, then the choice of metaphor carries a weight the decorative view cannot see. To cast argument as war, disease as an invader, or the mind as a machine is to frame a whole subject so that some questions come naturally and others fall into shadow, shaping our reasoning without our consent. We cannot think without such figures, any more than without concepts; what we can do is hold them a little more lightly, alert to what each one shows us and to what it quietly leaves in the dark.

passageSets.title: Are There Moral Facts
passageSets.passage:
When we say that cruelty is wrong, are we stating a fact, something true independently of what anyone thinks, or merely expressing an attitude, however deeply felt? The question divides those who hold that moral claims can be objectively true from those who deny it, and the stakes are high, for on the answer depends whether our fiercest ethical convictions describe the world or only our reactions to it.

The case for denying moral facts often begins with a comparison. The natural sciences discover facts by observation and experiment, and there is broad convergence about them across cultures and centuries; ethics shows no such convergence, only persistent disagreement, and no one has ever observed a moral property the way one observes a color or a mass. If moral facts existed, the argument runs, we should expect the kind of agreement and detectability that factual domains display, and their absence suggests that moral talk, for all its passion, is not in the business of describing a mind-independent reality at all.

Defenders of moral objectivity answer on several fronts. The disagreement, they note, is easy to overstate: across the widest range of cultures there is striking convergence on core matters, a shared condemnation of gratuitous cruelty, betrayal, and injustice, and much apparent moral difference turns out, on inspection, to rest on differing factual beliefs rather than differing values. As for detectability, they argue that the demand to observe moral properties with the senses simply assumes that all real facts must be like the facts of physics, an assumption that would also banish mathematical and logical truths, which no one observes yet few call unreal.

The dispute grows subtle because each side can accommodate much of the other's evidence. The objectivist can explain moral disagreement as the difficulty of a genuinely hard subject, no stranger than disagreement in history or economics; the anti-realist can explain moral convergence as the natural result of shared human needs and sympathies, no evidence of facts beyond us. What looks decisive to one side looks, to the other, like data to be reinterpreted, and the debate cannot be settled simply by pointing at agreement or its absence.

What is clear is that the stakes are not merely academic. If there are no moral facts, then to call an atrocity wrong is, at bottom, to voice a preference, powerful but not true, and the confident language of moral progress and moral error becomes hard to sustain. If there are moral facts, then our convictions can be right or wrong, and moral inquiry is a search for truth rather than an exchange of feelings. Neither conclusion is comfortable to hold consistently, which may be why so few manage to, reaching for the language of objective wrong even as they profess that none exists.

The stakes explain why so few can hold either conclusion steadily. If there are no moral facts, then to condemn an atrocity is at bottom to voice a preference, powerful but not true, and the confident talk of moral progress and moral error grows hard to sustain; if there are, then our convictions can be right or wrong and moral inquiry becomes a search for truth. Perhaps this is why even those who deny moral facts reach, in the face of real cruelty, for the language of objective wrong, as though the denial were easier to profess than to live.

questions.stem: The argument against moral facts based on the sciences claims that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: moral disagreement is easily overstated

choices.label: B
choices.text: mathematical truths are unreal

choices.label: C
choices.text: the absence of scientific-style convergence and observable moral properties suggests ethics does not describe a mind-independent reality

choices.label: D
choices.text: all cultures agree about ethics

questions.explanation: C is correct: the argument holds that if moral facts existed 'we should expect the kind of agreement and detectability that factual domains display,' and their absence suggests moral talk does not describe a mind-independent reality. A is wrong because overstated disagreement is the objectivist's reply. B is wrong because the objectivist, not this argument, raises mathematics. D is wrong because the anti-realist argument stresses persistent disagreement.

questions.stem: In reply to the 'detectability' point, defenders of moral objectivity argue that the demand to observe moral properties with the senses:

choices.label: A
choices.text: is obviously correct

choices.label: B
choices.text: proves that moral facts are like colors

choices.label: C
choices.text: wrongly assumes all real facts must resemble the facts of physics, which would also banish mathematical truths

choices.label: D
choices.text: shows that ethics is not a hard subject

questions.explanation: C is correct: defenders argue the demand 'simply assumes that all real facts must be like the facts of physics,' an assumption that 'would also banish mathematical and logical truths.' A is wrong because they reject the demand. B is wrong because they deny moral facts must be observable like colors. D is wrong because the difficulty of the subject is a separate point.

A is wrong because the author says the debate is unsettled. B is wrong because both sides marshal evidence. D is wrong because they disagree fundamentally.

questions.stem: In context, saying moral disagreement is 'no stranger than disagreement in history or economics' functions to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: show that moral disagreement disproves moral facts

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that history has no facts

choices.label: C
choices.text: support the objectivist claim that disagreement is compatible with there being facts about a hard subject

choices.label: D
choices.text: argue that ethics is easier than economics

questions.explanation: C is correct: the comparison lets 'the objectivist ... explain moral disagreement as the difficulty of a genuinely hard subject,' consistent with facts existing. A is wrong because the comparison defends, rather than disproves, moral facts. B is wrong because the point assumes history does have facts despite disagreement. D is wrong because relative difficulty is not the claim.

questions.stem: The observation that people reach 'for the language of objective wrong even as they profess that none exists' is offered to suggest that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the anti-realist position is easy to hold consistently

choices.label: B
choices.text: no one ever denies moral facts

choices.label: C
choices.text: consistently living without moral facts is difficult, since even their deniers speak as if such facts exist

choices.label: D
choices.text: moral facts have been decisively proven

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author says 'neither conclusion is comfortable to hold consistently,' evidenced by deniers using objective moral language. A is wrong because the point is that consistency is hard, not easy. B is wrong because the author acknowledges people do deny moral facts. D is wrong because the author does not claim the debate is settled.
